title: WiFi Hardware Components
description: Physical components that make up WiFi infrastructure — radios, antennas, access points, and controllers.
fullExplanation: WiFi hardware consists of a radio chipset (handles modulation/demodulation), antennas (transmit/receive RF signals), and a MAC controller (handles 802.11 framing, CSMA/CA, and association). Access points (APs) connect wireless clients to the wired network. Enterprise deployments use wireless LAN controllers (WLCs) to centrally manage hundreds of APs — handling roaming, RF optimization, and security policies.

components.name: Radio Chipset
components.description: Handles RF modulation/demodulation, OFDM processing, and PHY layer functions. Examples: Qualcomm QCN, Broadcom BCM, Intel AX series.

components.name: Antenna
components.description: Converts electrical signals to/from radio waves. Omnidirectional (360°) for general use, directional (Yagi, patch) for point-to-point links.

components.name: MIMO Chains
components.description: Multiple antennas for spatial multiplexing. 4×4 MIMO = 4 transmit, 4 receive antennas. More chains = more throughput.

components.name: MAC Controller
components.description: Implements 802.11 MAC: CSMA/CA, association, authentication, power management, QoS (WMM).

components.name: Access Point (AP)
components.description: Bridges wireless clients to wired network. Can be standalone (fat AP) or controller-managed (thin AP).

components.name: Wireless Controller
components.description: Centrally manages APs: roaming, RF planning, firmware updates, security policies. Examples: Cisco WLC, Aruba Mobility Controller.

apTypes.description: Self-contained — all intelligence on the AP. Simple home/small office use.
Minimal intelligence — tunnels traffic to WLC. Enterprise use for centralized management.
Managed via cloud dashboard. Examples: Cisco Meraki, Ubiquiti UniFi, Aruba Instant On.
APs communicate wirelessly with each other. No wired backhaul needed. Examples: Eero, Google Nest WiFi.

keyFacts: MIMO: 2×2 = 2 spatial streams, 4×4 = 4 streams — each stream adds ~300-600 Mbps
MU-MIMO: serve multiple clients simultaneously (WiFi 5 downlink, WiFi 6 uplink+downlink)
Beamforming: focus signal toward specific client — improves range and throughput
TWT (Target Wake Time): WiFi 6 feature — schedule client wake times to save battery
BSS Coloring: WiFi 6 marks transmissions to reduce unnecessary deferrals in dense environments
PoE powers most enterprise APs — no separate power outlet needed
